metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the 'Retry observability' section, state the logger name (e.g. `rebar.llm.runner`) and a concrete message substring (e.g. `"retry attempt"` for before_sleep and `"retries exhausted"` for the reraise path) so the caplog assertion in the test has an unambiguous, deterministic anchor.
findings.finding: The retry-observability AC ('Retry attempts + exhaustion are logged (asserted via caplog)') does not specify the log message pattern or logger name the caplog assertion should match. The `before_sleep` callback is described as logging 'attempt number + the wait it is about to sleep' but no concrete message format is given. An implementer writing the caplog assertion must invent the format, meaning the AC cannot be evaluated as written without a clarifying choice.
findings.location: AC item 7: 'Retry attempts + exhaustion are logged (asserted via caplog)'; 'Retry observability' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Retry observability': 'The before_sleep callback logs at WARNING under the `rebar.llm.runner` logger with a message matching `"LLM retry attempt {n}"` (attempt number) and `"waiting {w}s"` (sleep duration); exhaustion is logged at ERROR with `"LLM retries exhausted after {n} attempts"`. Tests assert caplog contains these substrings at the specified levels.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` applies `httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)` as the default per-attempt timeout when `http_timeout=None` is passed, so the 'never an unbounded stall' guarantee is tested before hoopoe lands.
findings.finding: The `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` helper's `http_timeout: httpx.Timeout | None = None` parameter and the guarantee that a non-None `httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)` default is always applied (so there is 'ALWAYS a per-attempt bound, never an unbounded stall') is described as a concrete deliverable in the 'Timeout semantics + the hoopoe merge interface' section and is named as part of this story's owned construction seam — but no AC verifies the helper signature, the default-timeout behavior, or that the per-attempt bound is present when hoopoe has not yet supplied an `httpx.Timeout`.
findings.location: 'Timeout semantics + the hoopoe merge interface' section; AC list (no matching item)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC list: '`_build_retrying_anthropic_model` uses `httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)` when `http_timeout` is `None` (asserted: the `AsyncClient` constructed by the helper carries a non-None timeout when no explicit `http_timeout` is supplied); the helper signature is stable for the hoopoe merge.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly whether the existing `model_settings['timeout']` line (runner.py:285-286) is removed, conditioned on `not using the new custom client`, or intentionally left as a redundant fallback when the unified helper constructs an `AnthropicModel` with a pre-baked `httpx.Timeout` — and state which layer governs the per-attempt timeout bound for retried requests.
findings.finding: The plan introduces an httpx.AsyncClient with a baked-in httpx.Timeout (defaulting to httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)) inside the new unified construction helper, while the existing runner.py:285-286 code simultaneously sets model_settings['timeout'] = float(cfg.timeout_s) for every Anthropic call. When both are active, the timeout is applied at two layers (the custom client's transport timeout AND pydantic-ai's ModelSettings timeout, which pydantic-ai also converts to an httpx timeout). The plan is silent on whether the existing model_settings['timeout'] line should be removed, conditioned, or left as a redundant-but-harmless belt-and-suspenders when the new client is in use — and which layer 'wins' is pydantic-ai-version-dependent internal behavior, not specified by the plan.
findings.location: Plan section 'Timeout semantics + the hoopoe merge interface'; runner.py:285-286
findings.suggested_fix: When the unified helper constructs the AnthropicModel with a pre-configured AsyncClient carrying httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s), remove or skip the model_settings['timeout'] assignment for that path (condition it on `not isinstance(model, AnthropicModel)`), so the per-attempt timeout is owned exclusively by the transport-layer client and the hoopoe interface has a single unambiguous source of truth.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the ADR (or a code comment on validate_response) that the status set is Anthropic-scoped and must be revisited if _build_retrying_anthropic_model is extended to non-Anthropic providers; this makes the hardcoding an explicit, documented choice rather than a silent assumption.
findings.finding: The retriable HTTP status set {429,529,500,502,503,504} is intentionally hardcoded in the validate_response callback with no extension point; the plan's justification covers current Anthropic-only scope, but the construction helper (_build_retrying_anthropic_model) is positioned as a reusable seam (hoopoe passes into it), so if a second provider is threaded through the same helper the status set cannot be adapted without a code change.
findings.location: 'The retriable status set {429,529,500,502,503,504} is intentionally FIXED in code' / Notes section / _build_retrying_anthropic_model helper
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment above validate_response: '# Status set is Anthropic-scoped (529 = Anthropic overload); revisit before reusing this helper for other providers.' Reference this comment in the ADR's Consequences section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum a `logger.warning('httpx.AsyncClient.aclose failed: %s', exc)` (or equivalent) inside the bare except in the aclose try-except block so cleanup failures are visible in logs, satisfying T5b observability for this failure point.
findings.finding: The `anyio.run(http_client.aclose)` in the try/finally is wrapped in a bare try-except that silently swallows all exceptions ('best-effort/try-except so cleanup never fails the run'). If aclose itself fails (e.g., event-loop teardown error, socket-level error), the failure is invisible — no log, no metric, no warning — violating T5b's observability requirement for new failure points.
findings.location: Notes on prior art — 'AsyncClient lifecycle (concrete)' section: 'the mechanism is best-effort/try-except so cleanup never fails the run'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bare `except: pass` (or equivalent) with `except Exception as exc: logger.warning('LLM httpx client close failed (best-effort): %s', exc, exc_info=True)` so that aclose failures appear in caplog and production logs without propagating to fail the run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `model_override=<AnthropicModel instance>` callers are intentionally excluded from the retrying construction, and if so, add a guard test or a note explaining why that override path never carries real traffic.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the offline `model_override` test seam is a NON-anthropic FunctionModel' as justification for why the unified construction helper is safe to apply unconditionally to all non-override paths. The actual test seam uses `pydantic_ai.models.function.FunctionModel` (verified in test files), and the guard `self._model_override is None` correctly bypasses it. However, the plan never specifies what happens if a future caller passes an `AnthropicModel` instance as `model_override` — the guard `self._model_override is None` would skip the retrying construction, silently giving that path no retry. This is a subtle scope gap: the plan says 'the unified helper covers BOTH real paths' but the override=AnthropicModel case (a legitimate test-with-real-model scenario) would be silently excluded.
findings.location: Plan section 'The single unified Anthropic construction'; guard: `self._model_override is None and resolved.startswith('anthropic')`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm explicitly in the AC or implementation notes that the `httpx.AsyncClient` is constructed inside `PydanticAIRunner.run()` (not `__init__`), so each concurrent `run()` invocation from the ThreadPoolExecutor gets its own client instance.
findings.finding: The plan states 'pass-1 runs finders in a ThreadPoolExecutor and each `run_sync` needs its own loop-bound client, so per-run construction is required for thread-safety.' This is the rationale for NOT caching the `httpx.AsyncClient`. However, the ThreadPoolExecutor fan-out is in `plan_review/pass1.py` — each worker calls `runner.run()`, and the plan implies each invocation of `PydanticAIRunner.run()` constructs a fresh client. But `PydanticAIRunner` is instantiated ONCE (as `self._config` is set at `__init__`) and `.run()` is called repeatedly. The plan must specify that the `httpx.AsyncClient` is created inside `.run()` (not `__init__`), otherwise a single shared instance created at `__init__` would be shared across concurrent workers — the exact thread-safety problem the plan claims to avoid.
findings.location: Plan section 'AsyncClient lifecycle (concrete)'; Notes section paragraph about ThreadPoolExecutor
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'The `httpx.AsyncClient` is constructed inside `PydanticAIRunner.run()` (not `__init__`); a test with two concurrent `run()` calls on the same runner instance asserts each gets its own client.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/local-dev-env.md`, `docs/llm-framework.md`, `src/rebar/_optional.py`, and `README.md` to the plan's Files section (or explicitly note them as doc-only follow-ons) since all hardcode `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]` and will need updating when the dep gains `[retries]`.
findings.finding: The dependency string change from `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]>=1,<2` to `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]>=1,<2` in `pyproject.toml:108` has unenumerated consumer doc files that hardcode the old string: `docs/local-dev-env.md` (2 occurrences), `docs/llm-framework.md` (2 occurrences), `src/rebar/_optional.py` (docstring), and `README.md`. The plan's file list omits these targets.
findings.suggested_fix: Add the four files to the Files list in the plan and include a task to update the hardcoded `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]` → `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]` string in each.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `RetryConfig` parameter introspection to the experiment log (or inline the captured output) to confirm `before_sleep` is an accepted kwarg; if it is not, document the alternative observability hook (e.g. a tenacity `before_sleep` wired through `config.before_sleep` or a custom `validate_response` wrapper).
findings.finding: The experiment log [3] shows `AsyncTenacityTransport` params as `['config', 'wrapped', 'validate_response']`, but the plan separately asserts `RetryConfig(..., before_sleep=cb)` is accepted ('VERIFIED'). The logged experiment output does NOT include `RetryConfig` parameter introspection — only the transport's params are shown. If `RetryConfig` is pydantic-ai's own wrapper, its accepted kwargs are a distinct surface; if `before_sleep` is not forwarded through to tenacity's `Retrying`, the observability callback silently drops without error.
findings.location: Plan section 'Retry observability' and 'Verified library behavior' experiment log [3]

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC item 2) that exercises the unified retrying-client code path end-to-end against the live Anthropic endpoint — e.g. a smoke/integration test (gated by a `RUN_LIVE_TESTS` env flag) that runs one real agent turn and asserts the retrying transport was invoked (via the construction guard triggering on a live `AsyncAnthropic` instance), confirming the wiring holds in the real network path, not only in mocked offline scenarios.
findings.finding: The plan's cutover to the unified retrying client covers ALL real Anthropic paths (normal + loopback), but no AC exercises this new production code path end-to-end against the live Anthropic endpoint. Every proving command in the ACs goes through offline/mocked transports (spike is explicitly 'no billable call'; all retry-behavior tests inject fake responses). If the transport wiring has an environment-specific failure (SDK version skew, httpx client lifecycle bug in the real network path, transport not actually applied in the non-test codepath), offline tests will still go green — the construction guard asserts configuration but not that a real Anthropic call flows through the retrying transport.
findings.location: ACs — items 2 ('spike integration test'), 3 ('construction-time guard'), 5 ('mid-run transient'); 'Verified library behavior' spike section (explicitly 'no billable call')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: '[ ] A live-smoke integration test (opt-in via REBAR_RUN_LIVE_TESTS=1) runs one real agent turn via the unified helper, asserts the construction guard fires (max_retries==0, transport present on the live AsyncAnthropic client), and the turn completes successfully — proving the transport wiring holds end-to-end, not only offline.' Gate it so CI doesn't bill on every push, but it must pass before the story is signed off.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the ADR stub must include at minimum: (1) the decision (transport-layer retry, SDK max_retries=0), (2) the rationale (side-effect-safety — completed tool calls not re-executed), (3) the rejected alternative (agent-loop retry), and (4) the Anthropic-specific scope note; update the AC to assert these sections exist, not just that the file exists.
findings.finding: The plan commits only to an ADR 'stub' without specifying minimum required content; a stub that omits the core decision rationale (why transport-layer retry rather than agent-loop retry, why SDK max_retries=0, side-effect-safety argument) fails to satisfy the T5e requirement that a novel architectural decision be captured — future maintainers need the rationale, not just the placeholder.
findings.location: ADR section / docs/adr/00XX-transport-retry.md stub / AC: 'an ADR stub exists at the placeholder path'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ADR AC: 'The stub contains sections: Context, Decision, Rationale (side-effect-safety argument), Alternatives Considered (agent-loop retry), and Consequences/Scope (Anthropic-only); the 00XX placeholder is replaced with the assigned number at integration.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` is a documented, stable public API in pydantic-ai's 1.x stability contract (not an internal implementation detail), and add a `make test` import-smoke assertion or pin a tested floor (e.g. `>=1.107,<2`) so a version bump that moves this symbol fails CI rather than runtime.
findings.finding: pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport is verified at pydantic-ai 1.107.0, but the plan pins >=1,<2, allowing any 1.x minor. The symbol lives in what appears to be an internal module path (pydantic_ai.retries) rather than a documented, stable public API surface — pydantic-ai's own changelog and API-stability docs may not guarantee this path across 1.x minor bumps. If the module is reorganized or the class renamed in a future 1.x, the construction-time guard catches it at runtime but the breakage is silent in CI until a pydantic-ai upgrade occurs.
findings.location: Dependency section / pyproject.toml:108 / Verified library behavior section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (and enforce in the AC) that all tenacity symbols used in `runner.py` are imported through `pydantic_ai.retries` re-exports (not directly from `tenacity`), consistent with the existing Burr tripwire ban on `tenacity` in the workflow layer, and add a note that the tripwire scope covers only executor+interpreter (so runner.py is currently safe but the pattern should be deliberate).
findings.finding: The codebase has an existing Burr tripwire (`tests/unit/workflow/test_executor_tripwire.py`) that explicitly bans `tenacity` (by name) from `executor.py` and `interpreter.py` via AST import scanning. The plan adds `tenacity` as a transitive dependency via `pydantic_ai.retries` and uses it inside `runner.py`, which is imported (lazily) from interpreter-level code. The plan does not acknowledge this constraint or confirm that no new direct `import tenacity` or `from tenacity import …` appears in `runner.py` itself (vs. being encapsulated inside pydantic_ai internals). If the implementation directly imports tenacity symbols in runner.py and the tripwire scope is ever widened to include runner.py, or if a future refactor pulls retry logic into interpreter.py, the tripwire fires.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `from pydantic_ai.retries import AsyncTenacityTransport, RetryConfig, wait_retry_after` exclusively; verify that `retry_if_exception_type`, `stop_after_attempt`, `wait_exponential` are re-exported from `pydantic_ai.retries` (experiment log does not confirm this), else import them from `tenacity` with a comment noting the tripwire exemption.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the mechanism for closing the `httpx.AsyncClient` (an async resource) from the synchronous caller after `run_sync()` has already created and destroyed its own asyncio event loop, leaving the lifecycle contract under-specified and the 'no unclosed-client warning' AC potentially unachievable with a naive try/finally.
findings.location: ## What / AsyncClient lifecycle section; AC 'AsyncClient does not leak (no unclosed-client warning across a run)'
findings.suggested_fix: Pass the `httpx.AsyncClient` into `AsyncAnthropic(http_client=...)` and do NOT independently aclose it — the Anthropic SDK (and pydantic-ai via `AnthropicProvider`) owns the lifecycle of a client it received and will close it when the provider is garbage-collected, which is bounded to the run since the model is constructed per-run. Add a comment explaining this ownership transfer. Alternatively, wrap the entire per-run block (client construction + agent.run_sync() + client close) in a single `asyncio.run()` call, but this requires restructuring the sync entry point.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
